race.name: El Tour de Tucson
race.tagline: 102 miles through the Sonoran Desert. Saguaro cacti, four mountain ranges, and the best November cycling weather in America. The season closer.

vitals.registration: Online via eltourdetucson.org. Early-bird pricing available. $85-130 depending on distance and registration date. Platinum (competitive 102-mile) and standard categories.
vitals.entry_fee: $85-130 (varies by distance and registration date; Platinum 102-mile at higher tier)
vitals.feed_zones: 8-10 aid stations on the 102-mile route, approximately every 7-12 miles. Staffed with water, bananas, oranges, cookies, pretzels, port-o-pots, and medical support.
vitals.cutoff_time: All routes close at 4:30 PM. Aid stations close on a rolling schedule to accommodate the deadline.

climate.description: November in Tucson is arguably the best cycling weather in America. Daytime temperatures of 49-71°F (9-22°C) with low humidity, clear skies, and abundant sunshine. The Sonoran Desert in autumn is comfortable and dry. This is the event's greatest asset — a warm, dry escape when most of the country has packed the bikes away for winter.
challenges: Morning starts can be cool (45-55°F) — arm warmers and a vest useful for the first hour
Sun exposure despite moderate temperatures — desert UV at 2,400 feet is intense
Dry air requires proactive hydration despite comfortable temperatures
Occasional wind on exposed desert sections, especially the southern return leg

terrain.primary: Flat to gently rolling Sonoran Desert
terrain.surface: Paved city and county roads circling greater Tucson. The terrain is predominantly flat to gently rolling with 3,007 feet of climbing across 102 miles. The course is a perimeter loop starting and finishing at the Tucson Convention Center, passing through Sonoran Desert landscape with saguaro cacti, mesquite, palo verde, and views of four surrounding mountain ranges.
features: Flat to gently rolling — 3,007 ft of climbing across 102 miles
Sonoran Desert scenery — saguaro cacti, desert flora, mountain backdrop
Four mountain ranges visible: Santa Catalina, Rincon, Tucson, Santa Rita
Paved roads throughout — no technical demands
Passes through/near Saguaro National Park, Old Spanish Trail, Davis-Monthan AFB, Colossal Cave area

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 57. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 57.

logistics.lodging_strategy: Hotels within 1 mile of Tucson Convention Center include Leo Kent Hotel (0.2 mi), Hampton Inn & Suites (0.3 mi), Home2 Suites (0.3 mi), and Ramada by Wyndham (0.4 mi), ranging $115-230/night. Airport-area hotels (Courtyard by Marriott, Four Points by Sheraton) run $99-199/night. JW Marriott Starr Pass Resort (6 mi) is the premium option. Book early for race weekend but options are plentiful — Tucson has abundant lodging at reasonable Southwest prices.

biased_opinion.summary:
El Tour de Tucson is the ride you do in November when everyone else has put their bikes away. The course is flat, the terrain is non-technical, and the climbing is negligible — 3,007 feet across 102 miles barely registers. None of that matters. What matters is riding 100+ miles through the Sonoran Desert in perfect 65-degree sunshine, with saguaro cacti and four mountain ranges as your backdrop, while your friends back east are staring at their trainers. The event's genius is timing: it's a season-ending celebration disguised as a century ride, and 9,000+ riders show up every year because cycling in November sunshine is a gift that Tucson delivers reliably.

The other thing El Tour gets right is accessibility. This is not a suffer-fest designed to break you. It's a community ride that welcomes first-time century riders alongside Platinum-category racers who finish in under 4 hours. The charity backbone — $130 million raised since 1983 — gives the whole weekend a sense of purpose beyond personal achievement. You're riding for something bigger, through a landscape most cyclists never see, in weather most cyclists can only dream about in late November. The competitive front end has attracted names like Chloe Dygert and Damiano Cunego, but the heart of El Tour is the 7,000 riders behind them who just want one more great day on the bike before winter.

final_verdict.one_liner: 102 miles through the Sonoran Desert in perfect November weather. America's season closer.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one last great outdoor ride before winter. Yes if you want a well-organized century in beautiful weather with a massive community and charitable purpose. Skip if you need climbing, technical terrain, or serious competitive racing — this is a participatory celebration, not a suffer-fest.
alternatives: Hotter'N Hell Hundred — if you want a hot-weather century with more suffering (August in Wichita Falls, Texas)
Tour de Palm Springs — if you want a similar desert century with a charity focus
Cape Town Cycle Tour — if you want a massive international road ride with comparable scenery and community

videos.title: A 'Tour' de force: Tucsonans take home first in premier men's, women's El Tour de Tucson races
videos.description: Over 11,000 cyclists from around the globe pedaled through Tucson roads Saturday for the 42nd Annual El Tour de Tucson, in what has become one of the region’s hallmark fitness and community events.
videos.transcript:
Over 11,000 cyclists hailing from all
around the world made their way downtown
and braved cooler than normal
temperatures. All to be a part of the
42nd annual Elour to Tucson.
>> This is the 42nd Elour to Tucson.
>> Riders woke up early to make the start
of the Century Race.
>> I got up at 4,
>> a 100mile route that took riders across
the Tucson metro region.
>> I mean, that's the more exciting one.
That's where that's like the the premier
um category to do. So, that's the one
where all the excitement, all the fun
happens there.
>> There were other races, too, like the 62
and 32 mile races, a 5K run, and a
three-mile fun ride where, you guessed
it, the riders were out to have some
fun.
>> I like feeling I know it's going to
sound kind of cheesy, but I like feeling
the wind on my face. It's refreshing.
After all the other races had kicked
off, the top riders like Steven Schaefer
of Tucson, who won the men's century,
sped to the finish line at the Tucson
Convention Center.
>> It means everything. It It feels like a
fairy tale come true. I mean, I I
visualized this. I I wanted it so badly.
>> Women's champion Marjgerie Ronaldo, who
won her second El Tour, said she was
happy to see Tissson's exposed to
cycling.
>> Everyone can race it. So, that's really
cool. like everyone who like some of my
co-workers like, "Oh, maybe I'll do the
32 mileer." And like they can do it. So
that's really nice.
>> From the Tucson Convention Center, Eddie
Sallayia, KA 9.

videos.title: Can We Survive The Biggest Bike Race In America?
videos.description:
Ollie and Hank decided to enter the biggest bike race in America, El Tour de Tucson. At 100 miles long, this was a test of endurance, but also speed. They tried to break the 4-hour 100-mile time barrier. Will Ollie and Hank manage to hit their target or fall behind the peloton?  

In association with https://gcn.eu/Visittucson 👉 @VisitTucson 
Check out our route 👉 https://gcn.eu/aam

00:00 Intro 
00:56 Race stats
02:45 Race start 
04:47 Break away
09:01 120km in 
10:00 Hank attacks 
11:10 Race finish

The Global Cycling Network (GCN) is the largest online cycling channel in the world, bringing together a global community of road cyclists to celebrate everything that’s great about the world of cycling.

Our videos bring fans compelling daily content including expert tutorials, techniques, training, racing, cutting-edge bike tech, unparalleled behind the scenes event coverage, humour,  entertainment, and more.

Presented by ex-pro riders, GCN offers a uniquely qualified insight into the world of cycling, and most importantly it’s fuelled by our passionate and enthusiastic fans – everyone who makes up the GCN community. We also bring the latest and greatest tech to your attention, showcase the best places in the world to ride and get exclusive access to events and races.

Welcome to the Global Cycling Network | Inside Cycling

videos.curation_reason: First-person race experience from established cycling channel with excellent production quality and high view count.

videos.title: Desert Cycling: 2023 El Tour de Tucson
videos.description:
A review of the 2023 El Tour de Tucson. My last race of the year!

Register here - https://eltourdetucson.org/
Bike rental - https://www.tucsonbikerentals.org/womens-rental-bikes/

music: blossom by ez
videos.transcript:
[Music]
oh
okay this is it this is my last race
review of the year and I'm so excited to
get into this one because it was so much
fun and definitely exceeded my
expectations but first hey guys my name
is I make videos on cycling and bike
commuting and today I just wanted to do
a review of my most recent race in
Tucson
Arizona so first I'll just give you guys
like a quick overview of what this trip
was this was a race in November it was
held on November 18th which was actually
my dad's birthday so this trip pretty
much serve double duty uh it was a
celebration of me completing my fourth
Grand bondle of the year
and also just to celebrate my dad's
birthday I rented a bike from a local
bike rental shop that was there I don't
think they have a physical shop but they
store their bikes in
storage near I think the loop which is
two sun's really long bike recreational
path I believe um this was pretty
expensive it was the most expensive bike
rental that I've paid for this year but
there weren't many of other options that
were great so this was pretty much
exactly what I needed they were super
professional really responsive whenever
I needed anything or had any questions
and added bonus was that they even set
it up to where they could pick up my
bike at the Finish Line once I finished
the race so I didn't even have to worry
about taking it back to the hotel where
I was staying at or using the rest of my
trip to figure out how to get back to
the storage location to return the bike
it was very seamless
and I don't really have any other
complaints about it it's kind of a you
get what you pay for type of thing so
more money meant a better
service but okay before I start rambling
too much I want to go ahead and jump
into the likes and dislikes like I have
in the other videos and first I'll start
with the dislikes for the El Tora Tucson
okay starting with number one which is
terrible roads oh my gosh I was not
expecting this but even my dad made a
comment about it while we were just
riding around in the rental car is that
their roads are pretty bad like it's
really rough shape I don't know if it's
because of the constant heat out there
but there were a lot of cracks and not
so many like huge potholes but just a
lot of cracks so there was a lot of like
rumbling and rough road and I was
concerned the days leading up to the
race that that was what most of the
roads would be like while we were out
there on the
bikes and I would say closer into the
city that was the case but the further
and further out we went it did get
better but there were some spots that
were still a bit shaky so if you plan on
doing this I just recommend that you
have some pretty good tires and that
they're inflated properly and that you
know how to change a flat just in case
because I just barely know how to do
it okay dislike number two the limited
variety of snacks now I will say that
they did have a fair amount of snacks at
all of the rest stops so it wasn't like
they didn't provide us with anything but
I have never eaten so many bananas in my
life in one day it was just nonstop
bananas and orange slices and maybe some
Oreos thrown in I could not take it
anymore like I just I had my gels and my
chews and all of that stuff but that was
also sugar so it was just like this
sugary taste in my mouth for so much of
the ride that I was craving salt of any
kind thankfully towards the end they did
have some stops that had pretzels with
big old things of salt on them and um
pickles and pickle juice so I was able
to get a salt fix in there some way but
yeah it every stop had pretty much the
same thing so if you're someone who
needs more variety then I suggest
packing that in your own stash and only
relying on their stuff when you don't
want to eat through all of yours too
quickly but yeah I was kind of hoping
for a little bit more variety but I was
still grateful that they had
snacked okay my third dislike was that
you have to decide your route before you
start and I'm sure some of you like
obviously that's what you do during
registration but what I mean by this was
that leading up to the race I realized
that I wasn't as prepared as I wanted to
be I kind of felt like I could do the
Sentry but was also feeling like I
should maybe bump down a little bit just
in case I couldn't complete the entire
route and I thought I could handle this
the same way that I did in Denver and if
you haven't watched that video go check
that one out I ended up switching routes
in Denver honestly unintentionally but I
was also able to make that decision for
myself while I was out there so I didn't
have to change much or go sign up for
something
different with this race that wasn't the
case I did have to make it clear before
I got out there on race day which one I
was setting out to do which I guess I
kind of get because there was a lot of
people this race is timed and they also
have like a Tracker thing on your like
race numbers that you put on your bike
and the metal that you get when you
finish is a different color depending on
which route you signed up to do now
normally when I do these lists I do four
likes and four dislikes but really this
race was so much fun that I couldn't
figure out a fourth
dislike so it's really more like three
which I guess could be the dislike is
that I couldn't even out the numbers I'm
telling you this was a really fun event
if you get a chance to do or even
considering doing it I say sign up it
was so much fun and really well executed
which is a great segue just to get us
into the likes of all the things that I
enjoyed about the race okay so with that
we'll just jump into the likes I guess
and my number one like was the great
views oh my gosh why did no one tell me
that Arizona is so beautiful like I just
had no clue I don't think I ever thought
of the state
previously uh but yeah the mountains
were
so amazing and they were in view the
entire time we were aside from the rain
we were pretty much blessed with
beautiful weather I think it was only
about like
75ish uh the whole time we were out
there and it was just amazing not as
beautiful as Cape Town so if you want to
see some of those views go check out
that video but this was beautiful in its
own right especially for something here
in the
US okay my number two like for elor to
Tucson was the Bike Expo this was
probably the best Expo that I think I've
been to it's a pretty good size and it
was very organized there was
entertainment there was food there's
lots of vendors there's a lot of
opportunities to grab things right
before the race uh there's not a huge
variety so make sure if you're
particular you have all the things you
know you like beforehand but if you need
to grab socks or gels or even a jersey
last minute they have those things a lot
of it is different merch that you can
get just to say that you did it so I
would say this was a pretty top rated
did expo well done my number three like
for the race was the traffic management
and support I think they did a fantastic
job of keeping cyclist separate from car
traffic and making sure that everyone
knew where to go there was no confusion
they also had bike Patrol if that's the
right term but people out on bikes
within the group of cyclists biking
around with extra tools and uh I'm
assuming first aid kits uh just things
to help
people in their time of need there were
quite a few people who had flats and
they were able to pull over and go help
them change a flat if needed they had on
I think it was yellow jerseys or
t-shirts or something on their bike so
they were easy to identify you didn't
have to really wonder if this is a
random person on the street or someone
who was part of the event so it was very
clear to spot them and there was even
one time when I started to get pretty
warm after the first 10 or so miles out
there on the bike so I I pulled over for
like 2 minutes to take off my sleeves
and somewhere in that amount of time or
less one of the bike Patrol people was
immediately over there asking if I
needed help or any assistance and so
that just goes to show how they were on
it when it comes to bike support so I
really appreciated that okay this last
one might be a little weird but I feel
like it helped a lot while I was out
there so I would say my last like of the
race was that the rest stops were
perfectly spaced
out the overall route and the terrain
out there in general is pretty flat so
you're pedaling a lot on this race which
can get very exhausting very quickly I
you pretty much was able to use all of
the rest stops as my opportunity to stop
and rest as opposed to taking my own
time while I was out there in between
stops to do my own rest obviously you
should rest when you feel like you need
to rest but for me making sure that I
stayed on Pace and on time with what I
wanted to
complete meant that I couldn't stop a
bunch of times but there was enough
spacing between one rest stop to the
next where I felt like okay this is a
good time to stop and it wasn't overrun
with people so I was able to get in and
out if I only wanted to stop for a
minute or two but if I wanted to stay a
bit longer then it wasn't overwhelming
or getting overcrowded to where there
wasn't space to be there okay so that
was it that was my review for the El Tor
to Tucson and also my last review for
the 2023 this little season I created
for myself this race was great and I
would highly recommend it to anyone
who's a beginner with longdistance races
like myself like I said it was way more
more beautiful than I expected it was
well supported extremely organized and
just an allaround good time I would
definitely do this one again I would go
for the century just to be able to check
that off the list but overall a really
great event that was extremely well done
and a whole lot of fun anyways that's my
review of el Tor to two sign I hope this
was helpful if you're considering
signing up for it next year definitely
do so as always I'll put the link to the
race as well as any resources that I use
that I thought would be helpful for
someone who's maybe considering doing
this next time I think if you do it you
will not be disappointed it was a lot of
fun this was the 40th anniversary of
this one so it was nice to get that big
metal that said 40 on it so yeah
definitely check this out and I hope
that you'll consider doing more of these
races as I try to do more of these races
and see more of what's out there on the
bike so pretty much since this the end
of the year I won't be doing anymore
until maybe next year and I haven't
quite figured out which ones those are
going to be yet but I'll definitely make
sure that I document them and provide as
much information as I can to help anyone
else who might be interested and doing
more of these races in the future so
with that said I just want to thank you
guys for watching my video and I will
see you in the next one
videos.curation_reason: Detailed race review with specific insights about course conditions and practical race experience.

quotes.text: It is an extremely fast route on big exposed roads with two key climbs or should I say drags in the route is typically completed by the front group in under 4 hours - this could be the fastest 102 miles we have ever ridden.

Terrible roads oh my gosh I was not expecting this - there were a lot of cracks so there was a lot of like rumbling and rough road. Closer into the city that was the case but the further out we went it did get better but there were some spots that were still a bit shaky.

It's literally insane it's like a festival of bike racing you got music and you've got literally thousands of riders that go as far as the eye can see and we're at the front of it and it's super exciting.

key_challenges.name: Pistol Hill
key_challenges.description: One of the big features where it all kicks off according to riders in the breakaway

key_challenges.name: City Roads
key_challenges.description: Terrible roads with a lot of cracks and rough surfaces, especially closer to the city. Roads improve further out from the city.

terrain_notes.text: Extremely fast route on big exposed roads with lots of cracks and rumbling, rough road surfaces especially near the city
Road conditions improve the further out you get from the city center

gear_mentions.text: Have some pretty good tires that are inflated properly due to rough road conditions with cracks
Know how to change a flat tire just in case due to road conditions

race_day_tips.text: Save your legs by surfing the wheels in the big peloton, cover attacks on the climbs
Fueling is going to be absolute key for maintaining 25 mph average over 102 miles
Hydration is critical because you are in the desert
Pack your own variety of snacks rather than relying only on aid stations which have limited variety - mostly bananas and orange slices
Bring salty snacks as aid stations are heavy on sugary foods

additional_quotes.text: They even set it up to where they could pick up my bike at the Finish Line once I finished the race so I didn't even have to worry about taking it back to the hotel
Riders woke up early to make the start of the Century Race. I got up at 4

rider_intel.search_text: El Tour de Tucson is described as an extremely fast 102-mile route with 2,885 feet of elevation gain, completed by front groups in under 4 hours at 25+ mph average speeds. The course features big exposed roads in desert conditions with two key climbs including Pistol Hill. Road conditions are rough with many cracks especially near the city, improving further out. Riders emphasize the importance of proper hydration in desert conditions and quality fueling for the fast pace. The race attracts over 7,000-11,000 participants making it one of the largest bike races in the US. Aid stations provide basic nutrition like bananas and orange slices but limited variety. The event has a festival atmosphere with music and massive participation. Cooler than normal temperatures were noted during recent editions.
